questions.q: What is the difference between git pull and git fetch?

answer.oneLiner: git fetch downloads the latest changes from the remote into your local tracking references — like origin/main — but it never touches your actual working branch, so it is completely safe to run anytime. git pull does the same download and then immediately merges those changes into your current branch, which can cause conflicts if you are not ready. Think of fetch as 'let me see what changed' and pull as 'download and integrate it now'.
keyPoints: git fetch updates your remote-tracking references such as origin/main so you can see what the remote has, but it leaves your local branch exactly as it was.
git pull is essentially fetch followed by a merge — it downloads the changes and integrates them into your current branch in one step.
On shared long-lived branches it is safer to fetch first, inspect the incoming commits with git log HEAD..origin/main, and then decide whether to merge or rebase.
Running git pull with the --rebase flag replays your local commits on top of the fetched changes rather than creating a merge commit, which keeps the history clean and linear.

code.snippet:
# Fetch only — safe, non-destructive
git fetch origin

# Inspect difference before merging
git log HEAD..origin/main --oneline

# Now explicitly merge
git merge origin/main

# Or: pull with rebase (linear history)
git pull --rebase origin main

# Set rebase as default pull strategy globally
git config --global pull.rebase true
code.note: Always fetch first on long-running branches to see what you are merging

dialogue.text: What's the difference between git pull and git fetch?
`fetch` only downloads remote changes and updates remote-tracking refs like `origin/main` — it doesn't touch my branch. `pull` does a `fetch` and then merges those changes into my current branch.
So after a fetch, where do the changes live?
In the remote-tracking branch, e.g. `origin/main`. My local `main` is untouched until I explicitly merge or rebase.
Say you fetch and see origin/main has 5 commits you don't — how do you safely review before merging?
I'd run `git log HEAD..origin/main --oneline` to see exactly what's incoming, then `git merge origin/main` — or `git pull --rebase` if I want linear history.

followUpsQA.q: What happens if you git pull with uncommitted changes?
followUpsQA.a: Git aborts the merge if it would overwrite local changes; otherwise it merges, but it can leave you with conflicts to resolve.

followUpsQA.q: How does `pull --rebase` differ from a regular pull?
followUpsQA.a: It replays your local commits on top of the fetched changes instead of creating a merge commit, keeping history linear.

followUpsQA.q: What is `git fetch --prune` for?
followUpsQA.a: It deletes local remote-tracking refs for branches that no longer exist on the remote.

redFlags: Saying fetch updates your working directory — it does not
Confusing origin/main with local main
Not knowing pull can be configured to rebase

questions.q: What is the difference between git merge and git rebase, and when should you use each?

answer.oneLiner: git merge combines two branches by creating a new merge commit that ties their histories together — the original commits are preserved exactly. git rebase takes your commits and replays them on top of another branch, rewriting their SHA hashes to produce a clean, straight-line history. A good rule of thumb is to rebase your own local feature branch before opening a pull request, then use merge to actually land it into the shared branch.
keyPoints: Merging creates a merge commit that shows where two lines of history came together, which makes it easy to see at a glance when a feature branch was integrated.
Rebasing rewrites each of your commits with a new SHA so they sit directly on top of the target branch — the result looks like the work was done sequentially all along.
The golden rule of rebasing is never to rebase commits that you have already pushed to a shared branch, because rewriting those SHAs breaks everyone else who has pulled them.
A common team workflow is to rebase a local feature branch to keep it up to date and clean, then merge it into main — often with the --no-ff flag to always record the merge event.
answer.detail: Rebase rewrites commit SHAs, so it breaks collaborators if used on published history. Typical flow: rebase your local branch before a PR, then merge (often `--no-ff`) to land it.

code.snippet:
# --- Merge approach ---
git checkout main
git merge feature/my-feature          # creates merge commit
git merge --no-ff feature/my-feature  # forces merge commit even if fast-forward possible

# --- Rebase approach (local branch cleanup) ---
git checkout feature/my-feature
git rebase main                        # replay feature commits on top of main
# resolve any conflicts, then:
git rebase --continue

# --- Interactive rebase: squash last 3 commits ---
git rebase -i HEAD~3
code.note: The --no-ff flag ensures a merge commit is always created, making it easier to revert a feature atomically

dialogue.text: Merge vs rebase — what's the difference?
Merge ties two branches together with a merge commit and keeps the full history. Rebase replays my commits on top of the target, giving a linear history but rewriting the commit SHAs.
When do you reach for each?
I rebase my own local feature branch to clean it up before a PR, and I merge to integrate into shared branches like `main`.
Your teammate already pulled your feature branch and you rebase it and force-push — what breaks?
Their history diverges from mine because the SHAs changed, so they get nasty conflicts. That's the golden rule — never rebase commits others have already pulled.

followUpsQA.q: What is a fast-forward merge?
followUpsQA.a: When the target hasn't diverged, Git just slides its pointer to the feature tip — no merge commit.

followUpsQA.q: How do you abort a rebase gone wrong?
followUpsQA.a: `git rebase --abort` restores the branch to its pre-rebase state.

followUpsQA.q: Difference between `merge --squash` and `rebase -i`?
followUpsQA.a: `merge --squash` stages all changes as one uncommitted change; `rebase -i` lets you fold and reword individual commits interactively.

redFlags: Rebasing a shared branch like main — rewrites public history
Not knowing rebase changes commit SHAs
Thinking rebase is always better than merge

questions.q: How do you handle and resolve merge conflicts in Git?

answer.oneLiner: A merge conflict happens when two branches changed the same lines of code in different ways and Git cannot decide which version to keep. Git marks the conflicting sections in the file with angle-bracket markers — your version, a separator, and the incoming version. You manually edit the file to the correct final state, remove those markers, stage the file, and then tell Git to continue the merge.
keyPoints: When a conflict occurs, Git marks the file with conflict markers: the section between the less-than signs and the equals signs is your current branch's version, and the section between the equals signs and greater-than signs is the incoming version.
You resolve the conflict by editing the file to its correct final state — this might mean keeping one side, the other, or combining both — then deleting all the marker lines.
After editing, you stage the resolved file with git add and then run git merge --continue or git rebase --continue to complete the operation.

You can abort the entire operation at any point with git merge --abort or git rebase --abort to return to the state before you started. Merging from the target branch frequently keeps conflicts small and manageable.
answer.detail: Use `git status` to find conflicted files and a merge tool (VS Code, `git mergetool`) for a 3-way view. Merge from the target branch often to keep conflicts small.

code.snippet:
# Check which files conflict
git status

# Open conflict markers in file — edit manually, then:
git add src/MyService.cs

# Complete the merge
git merge --continue
# or if you were rebasing:
git rebase --continue

# Abort and go back to pre-merge state
git merge --abort
git rebase --abort

# Use VS Code as merge tool
git config --global merge.tool vscode
git mergetool
code.note: Always run `git diff --staged` after resolving to confirm the file looks correct before committing

dialogue.text: Walk me through resolving a merge conflict.
First `git status` to find conflicted files. Git marks them with `<<<<<<< HEAD`, `=======`, and `>>>>>>>` — I edit each to the correct final state and remove the markers.
Then what?
I `git add` the resolved file and run `git merge --continue` — or `git rebase --continue` if I was rebasing.
Halfway through a big merge it's a mess and you want out — what do you do?
`git merge --abort` puts me right back to the pre-merge state. To avoid this I merge from `main` often so conflicts stay small.

followUpsQA.q: How do you use `git diff` around a conflict?
followUpsQA.a: `git diff` shows the conflicted hunks; `git diff --staged` confirms the resolution before committing.

followUpsQA.q: What is 'ours' vs 'theirs'?
followUpsQA.a: 'Ours' is the current branch's side, 'theirs' is the incoming branch's side — you can take one wholesale with `git checkout --ours/--theirs`.

followUpsQA.q: How do you reduce conflict frequency on a team?
followUpsQA.a: Smaller PRs, merging main in often, and short-lived branches so changes integrate before they drift.

redFlags: Deleting markers without actually choosing/combining content
Not knowing how to abort a merge
Blindly accepting ours/theirs without understanding impact

questions.q: Explain a branching strategy — describe Git Flow, Trunk-Based Development, or GitHub Flow and when you would choose each.

answer.oneLiner: Git Flow uses several long-lived branches — main, develop, release, and hotfix — and is well suited to products with scheduled versioned releases, like desktop or mobile apps. GitHub Flow simplifies this to a single main branch with short-lived feature branches and pull requests, which works well for teams deploying frequently. Trunk-Based Development is even more disciplined: developers merge very small, short-lived branches directly to main multiple times a day, using feature flags to hide work in progress, and it is the pattern associated with the highest-performing DevOps teams.
keyPoints: Git Flow maintains separate long-lived branches for development, releases, and hotfixes, which provides structure for teams that ship on a fixed schedule and need to maintain multiple released versions simultaneously.

GitHub Flow uses a single main branch where everything is always deployable — developers branch off, open a pull request, get a review, and merge. It is simple and works well for SaaS products that deploy continuously.

Trunk-Based Development keeps branches extremely short-lived, often less than a day, and relies on feature flags to hide incomplete features so the main branch is always in a shippable state — this is the preferred model according to the DORA research.

For a modern cloud-hosted .NET or Angular application that deploys frequently, either GitHub Flow or Trunk-Based Development is the pragmatic choice because Git Flow's extra branches add merge overhead without proportional benefit.
answer.detail: Git Flow suits app-store cycles and formal QA gates but adds merge debt. GitHub Flow needs strong automated tests since main is always deployable. TBD enables true CI and minimal integration risk but requires feature flags and good coverage.

code.snippet:
# GitHub Flow in practice
git checkout main && git pull
git checkout -b feature/user-authentication
# ... work, commit ...
git push -u origin feature/user-authentication
# Open PR → review → merge to main → CI deploys

# Git Flow — start a feature
git flow feature start user-authentication
# ... work ...
git flow feature finish user-authentication   # merges back to develop

# Trunk-Based — short-lived branch
git checkout -b feat/add-login-button         # lives < 1 day
git push && gh pr create --base main
# Merge same day
code.note: The git-flow CLI tool automates the branch naming and merging conventions of Git Flow

dialogue.text: What branching strategy would you pick for a .NET SaaS app and why?

For frequent deploys I'd go GitHub Flow or trunk-based — branch off `main`, PR, merge, deploy. Git Flow's long-lived develop/release branches add merge debt that doesn't pay off when you deploy daily.
What does trunk-based need to work safely?
Short-lived branches merged within a day, strong automated tests, and feature flags to hide work-in-progress so `main` stays always-deployable.
When would Git Flow actually be the right call?
Versioned software with scheduled releases and formal QA gates — like desktop or app-store products where you maintain multiple released versions and ship hotfixes to each.

followUpsQA.q: How would you implement a feature flag in a .NET API?
followUpsQA.a: Use `Microsoft.FeatureManagement` with a config/Azure App Configuration source and gate code with `IFeatureManager.IsEnabledAsync`.

followUpsQA.q: What are DORA metrics and how does branching affect them?
followUpsQA.a: Deploy frequency, lead time, change-fail rate, MTTR — short-lived branches improve lead time and deploy frequency.

followUpsQA.q: How do you handle hotfixes in trunk-based?
followUpsQA.a: Fix on a tiny branch off main, merge and deploy fast, often behind or via a flag — no separate hotfix branch needed.

redFlags: Only knowing Git Flow, unaware of trunk-based
Recommending Git Flow for multiple deploys per day
Not knowing feature flags are essential for TBD

questions.q: Explain git reset (soft, mixed, hard) vs git revert. When would you use each?

answer.oneLiner: git reset moves your branch pointer backward in history, effectively un-making commits — it rewrites history, so it is only safe on commits you have not yet pushed to a shared branch. git revert takes a different approach: it leaves history intact and instead creates a brand-new commit that applies the reverse of a previous commit. When you need to undo something that is already on a shared branch, revert is the right choice because it does not disrupt anyone else's history.
keyPoints: git reset --soft moves the branch pointer back but keeps all the changed files staged and ready to commit again — useful when you want to rewrap your last commit with a better message or structure.
git reset --mixed, which is the default, moves the pointer back and unstages the changes but keeps them in your working directory so you can review and re-stage them selectively.

git reset --hard moves the pointer back and discards all the changes entirely — it is destructive, but if you realize you need those commits back, git reflog can help you find the old SHA for roughly 30 days.

git revert creates a new commit that undoes the effect of a specific previous commit without touching any history, which makes it completely safe to push to a shared branch that others are working on.
answer.detail: Use reset for local unpushed commits, revert for anything already pushed. After an accidental hard reset, `git reflog` finds the lost SHA (kept ~30 days).

code.snippet:
# Undo last commit, keep changes staged
git reset --soft HEAD~1

# Undo last commit, keep changes in working dir (unstaged)
git reset --mixed HEAD~1   # or just: git reset HEAD~1

# Undo last commit, discard all changes (DESTRUCTIVE)
git reset --hard HEAD~1

# Safely undo a pushed commit (creates new commit)
git revert abc1234
git push

# Recover a SHA after accidental hard reset
git reflog
git checkout -b recovery abc1234
code.note: Never hard-reset commits that exist on a remote shared branch

dialogue.text: Explain the three reset modes versus revert.
`reset` moves the branch pointer back: `--soft` keeps changes staged, `--mixed` unstages them, `--hard` discards them entirely. `revert` instead adds a new commit that inverts an old one.
So which is safe on a shared branch?
`revert`, because it preserves history. `reset` rewrites history, so it's only safe on local commits you haven't pushed.
You ran `git reset --hard` and lost a commit you needed — recoverable?
Yes — `git reflog` shows the old SHA, and I can `git checkout -b recovery <sha>` to get it back. Reflog keeps entries about 30 days.

followUpsQA.q: How do you undo a merge commit with revert?
followUpsQA.a: `git revert -m 1 <merge-sha>` to revert relative to the first (mainline) parent.

followUpsQA.q: What is reflog and how long does it retain?
followUpsQA.a: A local log of where HEAD has been; entries are kept ~30 days (90 for reachable) before gc.

followUpsQA.q: Difference between `reset HEAD <file>` and `restore --staged`?
followUpsQA.a: They do the same thing — unstage a file; `restore --staged` is the newer, clearer command.

redFlags: Hard reset + force push on a shared branch — destroys history
Not knowing revert adds a new commit
Confusing --soft and --mixed

questions.q: What is a fast-forward merge and when does it occur?

answer.oneLiner: A fast-forward merge happens when the branch you are merging into has not moved forward since you branched off of it, meaning your branch is simply ahead on the same line of history. In that case, Git does not need to create a merge commit — it just slides the target branch's pointer forward to your latest commit. The result is a perfectly linear history as if you had always been working on one branch.
keyPoints: A fast-forward merge is only possible when the target branch has received no new commits since the feature branch was created — there is no divergence, so no merge commit is needed.
The advantage is a clean, linear history with no extra merge commits; the disadvantage is that you lose a clear record of when the feature branch was integrated.

Using the --no-ff flag forces Git to always create a merge commit even when a fast-forward would be possible, which many teams prefer on main or develop branches so the history shows when each feature landed.

The --ff-only flag does the opposite — it tells Git to only proceed if the merge can be a fast-forward, and to fail otherwise; this is useful in CI to enforce that branches are rebased before being merged.
answer.detail: Teams use `--no-ff` on main/develop to keep an explicit record of when each feature merged, which makes `git log --merges` useful for auditing.

code.snippet:
# Normal merge — fast-forwards if possible
git checkout main
git merge feature/login

# Force a merge commit even if fast-forward is possible
git merge --no-ff feature/login

# Fail if fast-forward is not possible (CI guard)
git merge --ff-only feature/login
code.note: GitHub's 'Squash and merge' and 'Rebase and merge' buttons both produce fast-forward-style linear history

dialogue.text: What's a fast-forward merge?
When the target branch hasn't gained any commits since I branched off, Git just slides its pointer forward to my feature tip — no merge commit, and history stays linear.
How do you force a merge commit anyway?
`git merge --no-ff` always creates a merge commit, which gives an explicit record of when the feature landed.
In CI you want to reject a merge unless it's a clean fast-forward — how?
`git merge --ff-only` — it fails if a fast-forward isn't possible, forcing the branch to be rebased/updated first.

followUpsQA.q: Why might a team always use --no-ff on main?
followUpsQA.a: To keep an explicit merge commit per feature, making `git log --merges` useful for auditing and easy atomic reverts.

followUpsQA.q: How does GitHub's 'Rebase and merge' relate to fast-forward?
followUpsQA.a: It replays commits onto the base so the result is linear, fast-forward-style history with no merge commit.

followUpsQA.q: What does --ff-only protect against?
followUpsQA.a: Accidental merge commits — it only proceeds if the branch can be fast-forwarded cleanly.

redFlags: Thinking fast-forward creates a new commit — it does not
Not knowing --no-ff can force a merge commit

questions.q: What is git stash and when would you use it?

answer.oneLiner: git stash is a way to temporarily save your uncommitted work — both staged and unstaged changes — and revert your working directory to a clean state, without losing what you were doing. It is like putting your work in a drawer so you can switch to something else, then pulling it back out later with git stash pop. This is really handy when someone asks you to review their branch or fix an urgent bug while you are in the middle of something else.
keyPoints: Running git stash saves all your staged and unstaged changes onto a stack and restores the working directory to match the last commit, so you can freely switch branches without committing half-finished work.

git stash pop brings back the most recently stashed changes and removes the entry from the stack; git stash apply does the same but leaves the entry in the stack in case you want to apply it to another branch as well.
By default git stash does not include brand-new untracked files that Git has never seen before — you need to add the -u flag to include those.
You can name a stash with the -m flag to give it a descriptive label, which is helpful if you accumulate multiple stashes and need to tell them apart.
answer.detail: It's a stack: each stash pushes on, each pop removes from top. Name stashes with `-m` when you accumulate several. Stashes survive branch switches.

code.snippet:
# Stash current changes
git stash push -m "WIP: user authentication"

# List all stashes
git stash list

# Apply most recent stash and remove it
git stash pop

# Apply a specific stash without removing it
git stash apply stash@{1}

# Include untracked files
git stash push -u -m "includes new files"

# Drop a specific stash
git stash drop stash@{0}

# Clear all stashes
git stash clear
code.note: Use named stashes (`-m`) when you might accumulate several; numbers alone are easy to confuse

dialogue.text: When would you use git stash?
When I'm mid-change and need to switch context fast — a hotfix on another branch, say. `git stash` shelves my staged and unstaged changes and resets the working tree to the last commit.
How do you get the changes back?
`git stash pop` restores the latest stash and removes it from the stack; `git stash apply` restores it but keeps it on the stack.
You stash, but a brand-new untracked file you created is still there — why?
Stash excludes untracked files by default. I'd use `git stash push -u` to include them.

followUpsQA.q: What happens to stashes when you switch branches?
followUpsQA.a: They persist — the stash stack is global to the repo, not tied to a branch.

followUpsQA.q: How do you stash only specific files?
followUpsQA.a: `git stash push <paths>` stashes just those files, or `git stash -p` to pick hunks interactively.

followUpsQA.q: Difference between stash pop and apply?
followUpsQA.a: `pop` restores and drops the stash; `apply` restores but leaves it on the stack.

redFlags: Not knowing untracked files are excluded by default
Confusing pop (removes) with apply (keeps)

questions.q: What is git cherry-pick and when should you use it?

answer.oneLiner: git cherry-pick lets you take a single specific commit from one branch and apply its changes onto a different branch, without bringing across the rest of the source branch's history. It creates a new commit with the same changes but a brand-new hash. The most common real-world use case is backporting a critical bug fix to a release branch when the full feature it belongs to is not yet ready to ship.
keyPoints: Cherry-picking applies the diff from one specific commit onto your current branch, creating a new commit with the same code change but a different SHA — the two commits are not linked in Git's eyes.
The classic use case is backporting a bug fix: your fix lives on a feature branch, but you need it in the release branch right now before the full feature is ready to merge.
If the commit you are cherry-picking depends on other commits that are not present in the target branch, you will likely get merge conflicts because the context Git expected is missing.

Avoid cherry-picking the same commit into many branches repeatedly because it creates duplicate-looking commits with different hashes, which complicates future merges and makes history harder to read.
answer.detail: Overusing it creates duplicate commits and diverging history — if you're picking many commits, do a proper merge or rebase instead.

code.snippet:
# Apply a single commit to current branch
git cherry-pick abc1234

# Cherry-pick a range (exclusive of A, inclusive of B)
git cherry-pick abc1234..def5678

# Cherry-pick without auto-committing (to edit before committing)
git cherry-pick --no-commit abc1234
git commit -m "backport: fix null ref in PaymentService"

# Abort a cherry-pick in conflict
git cherry-pick --abort
code.note: After cherry-picking a fix into main, don't forget to also merge main back into the feature branch so the fix is not duplicated on merge

dialogue.text: What does git cherry-pick do?
`git cherry-pick <sha>` applies the diff from one specific commit onto my current branch, without merging the whole source branch. It creates a new commit with the same changes but a different SHA.
Give me a real use case.
Backporting a single urgent fix to a release branch when the rest of the feature isn't ready to ship.
You cherry-pick a fix from a feature branch to main, then later merge that feature branch — any issue?
You can get a duplicate commit since the same change exists under two SHAs. I'd merge `main` back into the feature branch so Git knows the change is already there.

followUpsQA.q: Problems from cherry-picking the same commit into multiple branches?
followUpsQA.a: Duplicate commits with different SHAs and diverging history that complicates later merges.

followUpsQA.q: How is cherry-pick different from `git apply`?
followUpsQA.a: Cherry-pick records a new commit with authorship; `git apply` just applies a patch to the working tree with no commit.

followUpsQA.q: When would you cherry-pick over a full merge?
followUpsQA.a: When you need exactly one commit's change now and don't want the rest of the source branch.

redFlags: Cherry-picking large sets instead of a proper merge
Not realising cherry-pick creates a new SHA

questions.q: How do you squash commits and why would you do it?

answer.oneLiner: Squashing means combining multiple commits into a single one, usually to turn a series of messy work-in-progress commits into one clean, meaningful commit before merging a pull request. You can do it manually with an interactive rebase, or most code review platforms like GitHub and Azure DevOps have a 'Squash and merge' button that does it automatically. The result is a tidy main branch history where each commit represents a complete feature or fix rather than a trail of 'fix typo' or 'WIP' messages.
keyPoints: Interactive rebase with git rebase -i HEAD~N opens an editor where you mark which commits to fold into the one above them by changing the word 'pick' to 'squash' — Git then combines them and lets you write a unified commit message.

GitHub and Azure DevOps both offer a Squash and merge option on pull requests that automatically collapses all the PR's commits into one before landing it on main, without you having to do anything manually.

The alternative command git merge --squash stages all the changes from the source branch as a single uncommitted change, which you then commit yourself — it does not automatically create the commit for you.

The trade-off is that squashing discards the fine-grained per-commit history, which can make it harder to pinpoint exactly which change introduced a bug when using git bisect or reviewing an audit log.
answer.detail: Squash small features for a clean changelog on main; keep granular history for large multi-developer features where individual commits matter for auditing.

code.snippet:
# Interactive rebase to squash last 3 commits
git rebase -i HEAD~3
# In the editor:
# pick abc1111 Add login form
# squash bcd2222 Fix validation
# squash cde3333 WIP cleanup
# Save → edit combined commit message

# Alternative: merge --squash (stages all changes as one, you commit manually)
git checkout main
git merge --squash feature/login
git commit -m "feat: add login with validation"
code.note: git merge --squash does NOT create a merge commit — you must run git commit yourself

dialogue.text: How do you squash commits and why?
To turn noisy WIP commits into one meaningful commit for a clean history. I'd run `git rebase -i HEAD~3` and change `pick` to `squash` on the commits I want folded in.
Any easier way on a PR?
Yes — GitHub and Azure DevOps both have a 'Squash and merge' button that collapses the whole PR into one commit on `main` automatically.
What's the downside of always squashing?
You lose the fine-grained per-commit history, which matters for bisecting or auditing large multi-developer features.

followUpsQA.q: Difference between `merge --squash` and `rebase -i` squash?
followUpsQA.a: `merge --squash` stages all the branch's changes as one uncommitted change; `rebase -i` squash folds your own commits and lets you reword.

followUpsQA.q: After squash-merging a PR, what happens to branch commits in remote history?
followUpsQA.a: The individual branch commits aren't on main — only the single squashed commit; the branch is usually deleted.

followUpsQA.q: Why avoid squashing directly on main?
followUpsQA.a: It rewrites shared history, breaking everyone who has pulled it.

redFlags: Squashing commits on main directly (rewrites shared history)
Not knowing --squash leaves changes staged, doesn't auto-commit

questions.q: What is HEAD in Git, and what is a detached HEAD state?

answer.oneLiner: HEAD is Git's way of tracking where you currently are in the repository — it is a pointer that normally points to a branch name, which in turn points to the latest commit on that branch. When you check out a specific commit SHA or a tag directly instead of a branch, HEAD points straight at that commit rather than going through a branch, which is called detached HEAD state. The danger is that any new commits you make in this state are not attached to any branch and will eventually be garbage-collected by Git if you navigate away without saving them to a branch.
keyPoints: In normal use, HEAD points to a branch reference such as refs/heads/main, and that branch reference points to the latest commit — so HEAD is effectively two hops away from the actual commit.
Detached HEAD occurs when you check out a specific commit hash, a tag, or any reference that is not a branch name — HEAD now points directly at a commit with no branch in between.

Any commits you make while in detached HEAD state are unreachable from any branch name, meaning Git's garbage collector can delete them. To preserve that work you need to create a new branch pointing at those commits with git checkout -b new-branch.

If you realize you left useful commits behind in detached HEAD state, git reflog will show you the SHA of those commits for roughly 30 days, letting you recover them by creating a branch from that SHA.
answer.detail: HEAD is a pointer to a pointer — it usually tracks a branch, which tracks a commit. Detaching breaks that indirection, so new work isn't reachable from any branch until you create one.

code.snippet:
# Check where HEAD points
cat .git/HEAD
# → ref: refs/heads/main   (normal)
# → a1b2c3d4...             (detached)

# Enter detached HEAD (inspect old commit)
git checkout a1b2c3d4

# Save work from detached HEAD
git checkout -b feature/experiment

# Navigate relative to HEAD
git log HEAD~3..HEAD   # last 3 commits
git diff HEAD~1        # diff against previous commit
code.note: HEAD~1 = one commit back; HEAD~3 = three commits back; HEAD^ is equivalent to HEAD~1

dialogue.text: What is HEAD in Git?
HEAD is a pointer to my current commit, normally indirectly through a branch name — `.git/HEAD` usually holds `ref: refs/heads/main`.
And a detached HEAD?
That's when HEAD points straight at a commit SHA instead of a branch — it happens when you `git checkout <sha>` or check out a tag.
You commit some work in detached HEAD, then checkout main — what happened to that work?
It's now orphaned and unreachable from any branch, so it can be garbage-collected. I should have run `git checkout -b new-branch` first to keep it — or recover it via reflog.

followUpsQA.q: Difference between HEAD, ORIG_HEAD, and MERGE_HEAD?
followUpsQA.a: HEAD is current; ORIG_HEAD is where HEAD was before a risky op (merge/reset); MERGE_HEAD is the commit being merged in.

followUpsQA.q: How do you get back to a branch from detached HEAD?
followUpsQA.a: `git checkout main` (or `git switch main`); create a branch first if you want to keep detached work.

followUpsQA.q: What is origin/HEAD?
followUpsQA.a: A remote-tracking ref pointing to the remote's default branch, not your local HEAD.

redFlags: Not knowing detached-HEAD commits can be lost
Confusing HEAD with origin/HEAD

questions.q: How do you undo a commit that has already been pushed to a shared remote branch?

answer.oneLiner: The safe way to undo a pushed commit on a shared branch is git revert, which creates a new commit that applies the exact opposite of the bad commit — your history is preserved and everyone else's copies stay consistent. Force-pushing after a reset should be avoided on shared branches because it rewrites history that other developers have already pulled, causing their local branches to diverge and creating difficult conflicts for the whole team.
keyPoints: git revert creates a new reverse commit that cancels out the original one — because it adds a commit rather than removing one, everyone else on the team can simply pull the fix without any conflict.

A regular force push after git reset is highly disruptive on a shared branch because it rewrites history that other team members have already pulled down, forcing them to manually re-sync their local copies.
Reverting a merge commit requires an extra flag: git revert -m 1 followed by the merge commit's SHA, where the 1 tells Git to revert relative to the first parent, which is typically the main branch.

In the rare case where you absolutely must rewrite history — for example, if credentials were accidentally committed — use git push --force-with-lease instead of a plain force push, because it will refuse to overwrite the remote if someone else has pushed since your last fetch, giving you an extra safety check.
answer.detail: If you must scrub secrets from history, reset locally, force-push with `--force-with-lease`, have everyone re-sync, and rotate the secrets (assume compromised). `--force-with-lease` fails if someone pushed since your last fetch.

code.snippet:
# Safe: revert a pushed commit (creates new commit)
git revert abc1234
git push

# Revert a merge commit (specify mainline parent = 1)
git revert -m 1 abc1234merge
git push

# Dangerous: force push (only if truly necessary, e.g. secrets)
git reset --hard HEAD~1
git push --force-with-lease origin main
# Teammates must then:
git fetch origin
git reset --hard origin/main
code.note: Always prefer --force-with-lease over --force to avoid overwriting concurrent pushes

dialogue.text: How do you undo a commit that's already pushed to a shared branch?
`git revert <sha>` — it adds a new commit that inverts the bad one and I just push it. History is preserved, so nobody else gets disrupted.
Why not just reset and force-push?
Because that rewrites history everyone else has already pulled, causing diverging branches and conflicts. Force-push on shared branches is almost never the right answer.
What if the pushed commit was a merge, or it leaked a secret?

For a merge: `git revert -m 1 <merge-sha>`. For a leaked secret I'd scrub history with `git filter-repo`, force-push with `--force-with-lease`, have everyone re-sync, and rotate the secret since it's already compromised.

followUpsQA.q: How do you remove a secret from entire git history?
followUpsQA.a: Use `git filter-repo` (or BFG) to purge it, force-push, re-sync the team, and rotate the secret.

followUpsQA.q: How does `--force-with-lease` differ from `--force`?
followUpsQA.a: `--force-with-lease` refuses to overwrite if the remote moved since your last fetch, protecting concurrent pushes.

followUpsQA.q: How do you revert a revert?
followUpsQA.a: Run `git revert` on the revert commit to reapply the original change.

redFlags: Suggesting force push as the first solution
Not knowing revert exists or confusing it with reset
Not rotating secrets after a history rewrite

questions.q: What is CI/CD? Explain Continuous Integration, Continuous Delivery, and Continuous Deployment and the typical pipeline stages.

answer.oneLiner: Continuous Integration is the practice of automatically building and running tests every time a developer pushes code, so that integration bugs are caught early rather than piling up. Continuous Delivery means the pipeline keeps the application in a state where it could be deployed to production at any time, but a human still decides when to actually pull the trigger. Continuous Deployment goes one step further and removes that human gate entirely — every commit that passes all the automated checks is automatically deployed to production.
keyPoints: Continuous Integration means developers merge their changes frequently — often multiple times a day — and every push automatically triggers a build and a full test run so that broken integrations are caught immediately.

Continuous Delivery means the same automated pipeline produces a deployable artifact that is ready for production, but getting it to production still requires a deliberate human decision, often with an approval step.

Continuous Deployment removes the manual approval step entirely so that every build that passes tests is automatically released to production — this requires a very high degree of confidence in the automated test suite.

A typical pipeline flows through these stages in order: source control triggers the build, the code is compiled, unit and integration tests run, a security scan checks for vulnerabilities, the output is packaged into an artifact, the artifact is deployed to an environment, and then automated smoke or health checks verify the deployment succeeded.
answer.detail: The same immutable artifact (Docker image, package) is promoted through environments unchanged. Continuous Deployment requires strong tests, feature flags, and fast rollback.

code.snippet:
# Simplified GitHub Actions CI pipeline for .NET
# .github/workflows/ci.yml
# on: [push, pull_request]
# jobs:
#   build:
#     runs-on: ubuntu-latest
#     steps:
#       - uses: actions/checkout@v4
#       - uses: actions/setup-dotnet@v4
#         with: { dotnet-version: '8.x' }
#       - run: dotnet restore
#       - run: dotnet build --no-restore
#       - run: dotnet test --no-build --verbosity normal
#       - run: dotnet publish -c Release -o ./publish
#       - uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
#         with: { name: app, path: ./publish }
code.note: The artifact uploaded at the end is what gets promoted through staging and production — never rebuild between environments

dialogue.text: Explain CI, Continuous Delivery, and Continuous Deployment.

CI auto-builds and tests every change so integration bugs surface early. Continuous Delivery keeps that build always release-ready, but a human triggers the prod deploy. Continuous Deployment removes that gate — every green build ships to prod automatically.
What are the typical pipeline stages?
Source → Build → Test → Scan → Package → Deploy → Verify, promoting the same immutable artifact through each environment.
What has to be in place before you'd trust Continuous Deployment to prod?
Strong automated tests, feature flags to decouple deploy from release, monitoring, and fast automated rollback — otherwise a bad commit hits users with no safety net.

followUpsQA.q: Difference between a build artifact and a release?
followUpsQA.a: An artifact is the built output; a release is a specific artifact version promoted and deployed to an environment.

followUpsQA.q: How do you handle DB migrations in CI/CD?
followUpsQA.a: Run backward-compatible migrations as a separate step before deploy, using expand/contract so old and new code coexist.

followUpsQA.q: What metrics show CI/CD health?
followUpsQA.a: The DORA four — deploy frequency, lead time, change-fail rate, and MTTR.

redFlags: Confusing Continuous Delivery with Continuous Deployment
Thinking CI/CD is just running tests
Not knowing the same artifact deploys to all environments

questions.q: What is a build artifact and why should the same artifact be promoted across environments?

answer.oneLiner: A build artifact is the compiled, versioned output produced by your CI pipeline — for example a Docker image, a published .NET application folder, a NuGet package, or a JAR file. The key principle is that you build it once and then promote that exact same artifact through dev, staging, and production rather than rebuilding at each stage. This guarantees that the code you tested in staging is byte-for-byte identical to what runs in production, because rebuilding can introduce subtle differences in dependency versions or compilation flags.
keyPoints: An artifact is immutable once built — it should be tagged with a unique identifier such as the git commit SHA or a semantic version number so you can precisely trace which code is running in any environment and roll back to an exact previous version.

Promoting the same artifact across environments rather than rebuilding per environment is known as the build-once, deploy-many principle, and it eliminates an entire class of bugs where something worked in staging because a different library version was resolved at build time.

Environment-specific configuration such as connection strings, API keys, and feature flag settings should never be baked into the artifact itself — instead they should be injected at deploy time through environment variables or a secrets manager like Azure Key Vault.

Artifacts are stored in a registry — a Docker image registry like Azure Container Registry, a package feed like GitHub Packages or NuGet.org, or a file store — so that any environment can pull the exact version it needs and you have an auditable history of what was deployed when.
answer.detail: This is the 'build once, deploy many' principle from 12-factor. Store artifacts in a registry (ACR, GitHub Packages, Artifactory) so 'it works in staging' actually means something.

code.snippet:
# Build and tag Docker image with git SHA
GIT_SHA=$(git rev-parse --short HEAD)
docker build -t myapp:$GIT_SHA -t myapp:latest .

# Push to Azure Container Registry
docker push myregistry.azurecr.io/myapp:$GIT_SHA

# Deploy the SAME image to staging, then prod
# Deploy to staging:
kubectl set image deployment/myapp myapp=myregistry.azurecr.io/myapp:$GIT_SHA -n staging

# Promote SAME image to production (no rebuild)
kubectl set image deployment/myapp myapp=myregistry.azurecr.io/myapp:$GIT_SHA -n production
code.note: Environment config is passed as Kubernetes secrets or environment variables — never baked into the image

dialogue.text: What's a build artifact?
It's the compiled, versioned output of a build — a Docker image, a published .NET zip, a NuGet package. It should be immutable and tagged, usually with the git SHA or a semver.
Why promote the same artifact across environments instead of rebuilding?
Because rebuilding can pull different dependency versions or timing, so what you tested isn't exactly what runs in prod. 'Build once, deploy many' guarantees parity.
Where does environment-specific config go, then?
Injected at deploy time as env vars or secrets — never baked into the artifact, so the same image runs everywhere.

followUpsQA.q: How would you version a NuGet package in CI?
followUpsQA.a: Derive the version from git tags/GitVersion or the build number and pass it to `dotnet pack --version`.

followUpsQA.q: What is semantic versioning?
followUpsQA.a: MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH — major for breaking changes, minor for features, patch for fixes.

followUpsQA.q: How do you clean up old artifacts?
followUpsQA.a: Retention policies in the registry/feed — keep recent and tagged releases, prune old untagged builds.

redFlags: Rebuilding from source per environment
Baking env-specific config into the artifact
Not versioning artifacts, so you can't roll back

questions.q: What is the difference between blue-green and canary deployment strategies?

answer.oneLiner: Blue-green deployment runs two complete, identical production environments side by side and switches all user traffic from the old version to the new one in a single atomic flip — the old environment stays live so rollback is instant if something goes wrong. Canary deployment takes a more gradual approach: the new version is deployed and receives only a small fraction of real traffic first, and if the metrics look healthy you progressively route more traffic to it until the rollout is complete. Blue-green is faster to roll out but consumes double the infrastructure during the transition, while canary limits your blast radius but requires smart traffic routing and careful monitoring.
keyPoints: In a blue-green deployment you maintain two separate production environments and use a load balancer or DNS to switch which one receives all traffic — the swap is instant and rollback is equally instant by switching back.
The main cost of blue-green is that you need to run two full production environments simultaneously during the cutover, which roughly doubles your infrastructure costs for that window.

In a canary deployment the new version is deployed alongside the old one and initially receives a small percentage of traffic — say five or ten percent — and that percentage is gradually increased as you monitor error rates, latency, and business metrics to confirm the new version is healthy.

Both strategies require that your database schema and API contracts are backward-compatible at the point of the switch, because both versions of the application will be hitting the same database simultaneously during the transition.
answer.detail: Both run two versions at once, so APIs and DB schemas must stay backward-compatible. Azure: deployment slots = blue-green; AKS + Istio/Nginx weighting = canary.

code.snippet:
# --- Blue-Green: Azure App Service Deployment Slots ---
# Deploy to staging slot
az webapp deployment source config-zip \
  --resource-group myRG --name myApp \
  --slot staging --src app.zip

# Smoke test staging slot
curl https://myapp-staging.azurewebsites.net/health

# Swap slots (atomic, instant rollback available)
az webapp deployment slot swap \
  --resource-group myRG --name myApp \
  --slot staging --target-slot production

# --- Canary: Kubernetes with weighted ingress ---
# Route 10% to canary deployment
# nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/canary: "true"
# nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/canary-weight: "10"
code.note: Azure deployment slot swaps are atomic and warm — the slot is pre-warmed before swap, avoiding cold-start latency

dialogue.text: Blue-green versus canary — what's the difference?

Blue-green runs two identical prod environments and flips all traffic from blue to green at once. Canary gradually routes a small percentage of users to the new version and ramps up if it stays healthy.
What's the tradeoff?
Blue-green gives instant rollback but costs double infra during the switch. Canary limits blast radius but needs smart traffic routing and solid monitoring to decide whether to proceed.
Both run two versions at once — what does that demand from your DB and APIs?
Backward compatibility — schemas and APIs must serve both versions simultaneously, so I'd use expand/contract migrations rather than breaking changes.

followUpsQA.q: How do you handle DB migrations in blue-green?
followUpsQA.a: Make them backward-compatible (expand/contract) so both blue and green run against the same schema.

followUpsQA.q: What metrics do you watch during a canary?
followUpsQA.a: Error rate, latency, and key business metrics on the canary versus baseline before ramping up.

followUpsQA.q: How does a rolling deployment differ from canary?
followUpsQA.a: Rolling replaces instances one-by-one for everyone; canary deliberately routes only a subset of traffic to the new version.

redFlags: Ignoring DB schema compatibility in blue-green
Saying blue-green and canary are the same
Not mentioning monitoring as essential to canary

questions.q: How do you manage secrets in a CI/CD pipeline?

answer.oneLiner: The core rule is that secrets never live in source code — not even in private repos. CI platforms like GitHub Actions and Azure DevOps have encrypted secret stores where you save build-time tokens such as registry credentials, and those values are masked in any log output. For production, connection strings and API keys should live in a dedicated secrets manager like Azure Key Vault, and the application reads them at runtime using a Managed Identity so there are no credentials stored anywhere in the pipeline at all.
keyPoints: Never commit secrets to source control — use a .gitignore to exclude env files and add a secrets scanner like gitleaks to your CI pipeline to catch any accidental inclusion before it reaches the remote.

CI platform secret stores — GitHub Secrets, Azure DevOps variable groups — encrypt secrets at rest and automatically mask them in pipeline logs so they cannot be read from the build output even if a step prints environment variables.

For production workloads, use a proper secrets manager like Azure Key Vault accessed via Managed Identity so there is no long-lived credential to store, rotate, or leak — the application gets a short-lived token from the platform identity system.

OIDC federation, also called workload identity, is the modern alternative to storing service principal secrets in CI — GitHub Actions exchanges a short-lived JWT token for cloud access, meaning there is no static credential in GitHub Secrets that could be stolen.
answer.detail: Layers: no secrets in code → CI platform secrets for build-time tokens → runtime vault for prod connection strings/keys. If one leaks, rotate immediately and scrub history with git filter-repo.

code.snippet:
# GitHub Actions — using repository secrets
# steps:
#   - name: Deploy to Azure
#     env:
#       AZURE_CLIENT_SECRET: ${{ secrets.AZURE_CLIENT_SECRET }}
#     run: az login --service-principal ...

# Better: Use OIDC (no stored credentials)
# - uses: azure/login@v2
#   with:
#     client-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_CLIENT_ID }}
#     tenant-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_TENANT_ID }}
#     subscription-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID }}

# .NET: Read secrets from Azure Key Vault at runtime
# builder.Configuration.AddAzureKeyVault(
#     new Uri("https://myvault.vault.azure.net/"),
#     new DefaultAzureCredential());  // uses Managed Identity

# Scan for secrets in git history
git log --all | docker run --rm -i zricethezav/gitleaks detect --pipe
code.note: OIDC federation (workload identity) eliminates the need to store long-lived Azure credentials in GitHub Secrets

dialogue.text: How do you manage secrets in a CI/CD pipeline?

Never in source. Build-time tokens live in the CI platform's encrypted secret store, masked in logs. Prod connection strings and keys live in a vault like Azure Key Vault, read at runtime via Managed Identity.
How do you authenticate the pipeline to Azure without storing a credential?
OIDC federation — GitHub Actions exchanges a short-lived token for Azure access, so there's no long-lived service-principal secret sitting in GitHub Secrets.
Someone accidentally committed a connection string last week — what now?
Rotate it immediately, assume it's compromised, then scrub it from history with `git filter-repo` and add a scanner like gitleaks to CI to catch it next time.

followUpsQA.q: What is OIDC federation and why prefer it over SP secrets?
followUpsQA.a: Workload identity exchanges a short-lived token for cloud access — no long-lived stored credential to leak.

followUpsQA.q: How do you scope prod secrets to the prod pipeline only?
followUpsQA.a: Use environment-scoped secrets with approval gates so only the protected prod environment can read them.

followUpsQA.q: What do you do if a secret leaks into git history?
followUpsQA.a: Rotate it first, then purge history with git filter-repo/BFG and force-push.

redFlags: Committing .env files even in private repos
Not mentioning secret rotation
Not knowing Managed Identity / OIDC

questions.q: How would you set up a CI/CD pipeline for a .NET API + Angular frontend app using GitHub Actions?

answer.oneLiner: I would structure it as two parallel build jobs — one for the .NET backend and one for the Angular frontend — so they both build and test at the same time rather than sequentially. Each job produces an artifact that is passed downstream. On a merge to main, a deployment job automatically deploys both artifacts to a staging environment in Azure, and then a separate production job waits for a manual approval via a GitHub Environment before promoting the same artifacts to production. Authentication to Azure uses OIDC so there are no stored credentials in the pipeline.
keyPoints: Running the backend and frontend as separate parallel jobs significantly reduces total pipeline time — the .NET restore, build, test, and publish steps run at the same time as the Angular npm install, build, and bundle steps.

The backend job runs dotnet restore, dotnet build, dotnet test, and dotnet publish and then uploads the published output as an artifact; the frontend job runs npm ci and ng build with the production configuration and uploads the dist folder.

GitHub Environments give you a proper approval gate for production — you configure required reviewers on the production environment, and the deploy-to-production job cannot run until someone clicks approve, while dev and staging deploy automatically.

Database migrations with Entity Framework Core should run as a separate step before the application is deployed — using the expand-and-contract pattern to keep the schema backward-compatible so that the old application version can still run against the migrated database during the brief window of the rollout.
answer.detail: Promote the same artifact from staging to prod (slots for zero-downtime). Run EF migrations as a separate backward-compatible step before deploy. Rollback via slot swap or re-deploying the previous artifact.

code.snippet:
# Simplified .github/workflows/ci-cd.yml structure
#
# name: CI/CD
# on:
#   push:
#     branches: [main]
#
# jobs:
#   backend:
#     runs-on: ubuntu-latest
#     steps:
#       - uses: actions/checkout@v4
#       - uses: actions/setup-dotnet@v4
#         with: { dotnet-version: '8.x' }
#       - run: dotnet restore && dotnet build --no-restore
#       - run: dotnet test --no-build
#       - run: dotnet publish -c Release -o publish
#       - uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
#         with: { name: backend, path: publish }
#
#   frontend:
#     runs-on: ubuntu-latest
#     steps:
#       - uses: actions/checkout@v4
#       - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
#         with: { node-version: '20' }
#       - run: npm ci && npx ng build --configuration production
#       - uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
#         with: { name: frontend, path: dist }
#
#   deploy-staging:
#     needs: [backend, frontend]
#     environment: staging
#     runs-on: ubuntu-latest
#     steps:
#       - uses: azure/login@v2  # OIDC
#       - uses: actions/download-artifact@v4
#       - uses: azure/webapps-deploy@v3
#
#   deploy-production:
#     needs: deploy-staging
#     environment: production   # requires approval
#     runs-on: ubuntu-latest
#     steps:
#       - uses: azure/webapps-deploy@v3  # same artifact
code.note: Backend and frontend jobs run in parallel, cutting total pipeline time roughly in half

dialogue.text: How would you structure a GitHub Actions pipeline for a .NET API plus Angular frontend?
Two parallel jobs that fail fast — backend does restore → build → test → publish, frontend does `npm ci` → `ng build --prod` → upload dist. Both publish artifacts.
How does deployment flow from there?
On merge to main, auto-deploy the artifacts to Azure staging, then promote the same artifact to prod through a GitHub Environment with reviewer approval. Auth to Azure is via OIDC, no stored creds.
Where do EF Core migrations fit so a deploy doesn't break running pods?
As a separate, backward-compatible step before the app deploy — expand/contract so the old version still works during the rollout, and rollback is a slot swap or re-deploying the previous artifact.

followUpsQA.q: How would you handle EF Core migrations safely here?
followUpsQA.a: Run them as a pre-deploy step using expand/contract so old and new code both work during rollout.

followUpsQA.q: How do you block prod deploy if integration tests fail?
followUpsQA.a: Make the prod job `needs` the test job and gate the environment so it can't run unless tests pass.

followUpsQA.q: How would you add SonarCloud analysis?
followUpsQA.a: Add a scan step in the build job with the SonarCloud action and fail the build on quality-gate failure.

redFlags: Building backend and frontend sequentially
Rebuilding per environment instead of promoting one artifact
No environment protection / approval gate for prod
Hardcoding Azure credentials in the workflow

questions.q: What is an environment promotion strategy (dev → staging → production)?

answer.oneLiner: An environment promotion strategy means you take the same versioned, immutable artifact and advance it through a sequence of increasingly production-like environments, with automated and manual quality gates between each stage. Development gets every commit automatically. Staging gets every merge to main automatically and runs full integration and end-to-end tests. Production requires a deliberate human approval after staging is green. The key insight is that you are promoting the artifact, not rebuilding from source, so the code that passed your tests in staging is byte-for-byte what runs in production.
keyPoints: Each environment in the chain should be as similar to production as possible in terms of infrastructure, data shape, and configuration — if staging uses a differently sized database or different network rules, the tests it runs are not actually predicting production behavior.

The same immutable artifact is promoted through each environment rather than rebuilt at each stage; only the environment-specific configuration such as connection strings and feature flag values changes.

Automated gates between environments — unit tests, integration tests, security scans, and smoke tests — run without human intervention, while the final gate before production requires a manual approval from a team member.
Keeping the previous artifact available at each stage means you can roll back quickly to the last known-good version using a slot swap or a re-deploy command, without waiting for a new build.
answer.detail: Staging mirrors prod and runs integration/E2E/UAT as the final gate. Keep the previous artifact for rollback (slot swap or `kubectl rollout undo`).

code.snippet:
# GitHub Actions environment promotion (conceptual)
# deploy-dev:
#   environment: dev
#   # auto-deploys on every push
#
# deploy-staging:
#   needs: [test]
#   environment: staging          # auto, no approval
#   # runs integration tests after deploy
#
# deploy-production:
#   needs: [deploy-staging, integration-tests]
#   environment: production       # requires manual approval
#   # Uses SAME artifact as staging

# Azure CLI: promote same image through environments
IMAGE="myregistry.azurecr.io/myapp:$GIT_SHA"
az webapp config container set --name myapp-staging   --resource-group rg --docker-custom-image-name $IMAGE
# After approval:
az webapp config container set --name myapp-production --resource-group rg --docker-custom-image-name $IMAGE
code.note: The $GIT_SHA image tag ensures you can trace exactly which code is running in each environment

dialogue.text: What's an environment promotion strategy?

Advancing the same immutable artifact through increasingly production-like environments — dev auto on commit, staging auto on merge, prod behind a manual approval — with gates validating quality at each step.
Why keep staging as production-like as possible?
So integration, E2E, and UAT in staging actually predict prod behavior; if staging differs, 'it passed in staging' means nothing.
A bug slips to prod despite the gates — how does this setup help you recover?
I keep the previous artifact, so rollback is a slot swap or `kubectl rollout undo` to the last known-good version, with no rebuild.

followUpsQA.q: How do you handle PII differences between staging and prod data?
followUpsQA.a: Use anonymized or synthetic data in staging so it mirrors prod shape without exposing real PII.

followUpsQA.q: What is a 'release train'?
followUpsQA.a: A fixed schedule where whatever's ready by the cutoff ships together, decoupling release timing from feature completion.

followUpsQA.q: How do you coordinate promotion with DB migrations?
followUpsQA.a: Run backward-compatible migrations ahead of the app promotion so each environment's schema supports both versions.

redFlags: Rebuilding per environment
Staging significantly different from prod
No automated tests between dev and prod

questions.q: What is your rollback strategy when a production deployment goes wrong?

answer.oneLiner: The goal is to restore the last known-good state as fast as possible — ideally in minutes — without waiting for a new build. For Azure App Service that means swapping deployment slots back; for Kubernetes that means running kubectl rollout undo; for any platform it means re-deploying the previous versioned artifact that you kept in your registry. The hard part is not the application rollback but the database — if a schema migration was part of the deployment, you need a plan for that too, which is why I always use the expand-and-contract migration pattern so the previous code version can still run against the migrated schema.
keyPoints: Application rollback is straightforward when you keep previous versioned artifacts: redeploy the previous Docker image or published package, or use a platform feature like Azure deployment slot swap or Kubernetes rollout undo to switch back in seconds.

Database rollback is the difficult part — a destructive schema migration such as dropping a column cannot simply be reversed, which is why the expand-and-contract pattern exists: you add new columns and keep old ones in the same release, then remove the old ones in a later release after the code no longer references them.

Feature flags provide the fastest form of rollback for a misbehaving feature because you can flip the flag off in a configuration store and the feature is immediately disabled for all users without requiring any deployment or restart.

A rollback plan only counts if you have actually rehearsed it in staging — the time to discover that your slot swap takes fifteen minutes or that your rollback script has a bug is not during a production incident.
answer.detail: Backward-incompatible schema changes can't auto-roll-back; expand/contract adds the column first and drops the old one a release later. Rehearse rollbacks in staging and track rollback time as a KPI.

code.snippet:
# Azure App Service slot rollback (instant)
az webapp deployment slot swap \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myApp \
  --slot staging \
  --target-slot production

# Kubernetes rollback
kubectl rollout undo deployment/myapp -n production
kubectl rollout status deployment/myapp -n production

# Re-deploy specific previous artifact (GitHub Actions)
# Trigger workflow_dispatch with the previous git SHA:
# IMAGE=myregistry.azurecr.io/myapp:abc1234-prev
az webapp config container set \
  --name myapp-production \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --docker-custom-image-name myregistry.azurecr.io/myapp:abc1234-prev

# Check rollout history
kubectl rollout history deployment/myapp
code.note: Always test your rollback procedure in staging — a rollback plan you have never rehearsed is not a plan

dialogue.text: A prod deploy goes wrong — what's your rollback strategy?

Get back to the last known-good state in minutes — Azure slot swap, `kubectl rollout undo`, or re-deploying the previous versioned artifact. The app side is fast because I keep that prior artifact around.
What's the hard part of a rollback?
The database. A backward-incompatible schema change can't just be reversed, so I use expand/contract — add the new column first, drop the old one a release later — keeping rollback safe.
You need to disable a broken feature in seconds without a redeploy — how?
A feature flag — flip it off and the code path is disabled instantly, no rebuild or redeploy needed. And I rehearse rollbacks in staging so they actually work under pressure.

followUpsQA.q: What is the expand/contract migration pattern?
followUpsQA.a: Add new schema, migrate to use it, then remove the old schema in a later release — so each step is backward-compatible.

followUpsQA.q: How do you decide roll back vs. roll forward?
followUpsQA.a: Roll back if the fix is unknown or risky; roll forward if you have a quick, verified patch ready.

followUpsQA.q: How would feature flags disable functionality without redeploy?
followUpsQA.a: The flag is read at runtime, so toggling it off in config instantly skips the code path.

redFlags: 'Just redeploy old code' ignoring DB schema incompatibilities
No pre-written rollback runbook before deploying
Not knowing how long a rollback takes (= your MTTR floor)
